name: Cone-beam Breast CT Study
description: The dataset of breast CT scans using Koning system
details: The system uses a tungsten-anode mammography tube (RAD71SP, Varex Imaging) operating at 49 kV with a 1.4 mm HVL, and an amorphous silicon flat-panel detector (PaxScan 4030CB, Varex Imaging) with 768×1,024 elements (388×388 μm²). The nominal image matrix is 1,024×1,024, with slice count depending on breast size. Each voxel measures 273.43 μm³. There are 155 volumes in total—50 from Koning and 105 from the University of Arizona.

name: Albany Medical Center Deidentified ICU CT Dataset
description: A deidentified clinical CT dataset of critically ill ICU patients with associated demographic, clinical severity, outcome, and body composition measurements.
details: This dataset consists of deidentified CT scans from ICU patients treated at Albany Medical Center. Each CT volume is stored in a folder named by Subject ID, which directly corresponds to the Subject ID column in the accompanying metadata spreadsheet. The dataset includes ICU admission information, mechanical ventilation status and duration, ICU length of stay, 28 ICU-free days, and 180-day survival outcomes. Clinical severity scores (APACHE II, SAPS II, SOFA), demographic variables (age, sex, race), prehospitalization comorbidities, laboratory values at admission, and CT-derived body composition measurements are provided. Body composition features include pectoralis major and minor muscle areas, erector spinae muscle areas (postural muscle), subcutaneous adipose tissue area, vertebral body density (bone), and major vessel diameters. All data are deidentified, and multiple severity scores should not be used simultaneously due to collinearity, as noted in the data dictionary.
project: Deep Learning for Medical CT and Critical Care Outcome Analysis

name: Merlin Abdominal CT Dataset
description: A large-scale deidentified abdominal and pelvis CT dataset from Stanford Hospital Emergency Department, with each scan paired to its corresponding radiology report.
details: This dataset contains 25,494 abdominal and pelvis CT scans from 18,317 patients, collected from exams performed between 2012 and 2018 at the Stanford Hospital Emergency Department. Cases were selected using CPT codes 72192, 72193, 72194, 74150, 74160, 74170, 74176, 74177, and 74178 through the STARR tool. For each exam, the DICOM series with the largest slice count was selected and converted into compressed NIfTI format. Patient-identifiable metadata was removed during processing. Each scan is paired with its corresponding radiology report, making the dataset suitable for medical imaging, radiology report generation, multimodal learning, and abdominal CT analysis tasks.
project: Abdominal CT Imaging and Radiology Report Analysis

name: CT-RATE Chest CT Dataset
description: A large-scale chest CT dataset pairing 3D CT volumes with corresponding radiology text reports, abnormality labels, and metadata.
details: CT-RATE is a deidentified chest CT dataset designed to support computational research in 3D medical imaging and vision-language modeling. It contains 25,692 non-contrast chest CT volumes, expanded to 50,188 volumes through multiple reconstructions, from 21,304 unique patients. Each CT volume is paired with its corresponding radiology text report, along with multi-abnormality labels and metadata. The cohort is divided into training and validation groups, with 20,000 patients assigned to the training set and 1,304 patients assigned to the validation set. Folder names follow the structure split_patientID_scanID_reconstructionID. For example, "valid_53_a_1" denotes a validation-set CT volume from patient 53, scan "a", reconstruction 1. This naming convention is used consistently throughout the dataset.
project: Chest CT Vision-Language Modeling and Radiology Report Analysis

name: RadGenome-Chest CT Dataset
description: A large-scale grounded vision-language chest CT dataset with region-level segmentation masks, grounded reports, and grounded VQA pairs for chest CT analysis.
details: RadGenome-Chest CT is a comprehensive region-guided 3D chest CT interpretation dataset built on top of CT-RATE. It extends the original dataset of over 25,692 non-contrast 3D chest CT volumes and reports from 20,000 patients by adding multiple forms of grounded supervision for multimodal medical AI research. The dataset includes organ-level segmentation masks covering 197 categories, providing intermediate visual reasoning cues for interpretation. It also contains 665,000 multi-granularity grounded reports, where each sentence is linked to the corresponding anatomical region in the CT volume through a segmentation mask, and 1.3 million grounded VQA pairs, where both questions and answers are associated with reference segmentation masks. All grounded reports and VQA pairs in the validation set were manually verified to ensure dataset quality. This dataset is designed to support the development of multimodal medical foundation models that can connect regional visual evidence with textual explanations and generate region-grounded clinical text.
project: Grounded Vision-Language Learning for Chest CT Analysis

name: Bio-Imaging and Bio-Instrumentation Lecture Dataset
description: A slide-grounded medical imaging lecture dataset consisting of course lectures with aligned slide images and per-slide transcripts.
details: This dataset is derived from the course Bio-Imaging and Bio-Instrumentation and contains 23 lectures covering major medical imaging modalities, including X-ray radiography, CT, MRI, PET/SPECT, ultrasound, optical imaging, and deep imaging. Each lecture is organized as an ordered sequence of slide images paired with aligned per-slide transcripts. Across the full course, the dataset contains 1,023 slide-transcript pairs, with an average of 44.5 slides per lecture and a range of 38 to 56 slides. The dataset is suitable for educational AI, lecture-grounded question answering, multimodal learning, speech generation, and medical imaging instruction research.
project: Medical Imaging Education and Slide-Grounded Multimodal Learning
